chunks.text: All right, friends, Greg Kokel here, and thank you for tuning in. I guess tuning in is kind of a radio deal, right? Do people tune in to podcasts or whatever it is that we're doing here? Anyway, we're off schedule this week, so I won't be taking live calls. Actually, sometimes we do get a live call while... this is not a streaming item, though, right? No, so you can't connect with me unless by accident. The regular show, though, between 4 and 6 p.m., Pacific time, 855-243-9975, 855-243-9975, that gets you Amy, and Amy will get you to me if you're nice to her, and most of you usually are. Have you ever turned anybody down, Amy? I don't think you have. No, she just, whatever, just lets them in. Whoever will may come, right? It's the general call, as it were. So, since we're off schedule today, I will be taking open mic calls. Of course, this is when you call our website, kind of, not on a phone, but you just go to str.org, and then under, at the homepage, you'll see podcasts. Find that. Then under that, live broadcasts, and then you could leave a question. And then we'll eventually get to it. So, I usually try to deal with the oldest first. Now, you mercifully, Amy, have not put the dates next to your summary and the names, so I don't know how long ago this is, and we just get there sooner or later. But let's hear from Ed, all right? He's left a question. We'll hear what he had to say, and then I'll respond. Ed wrote... Hello, Greg. Ed Beis here. I've been glad for several years to be a strategic partner. I have a question that I think you may find interesting. We know that forgiveness is central to the Christian life, the way we're supposed to live. Jesus, in Matthew 6, 12, and 14 and 15, the Sermon on the Mount, is very clear about our responsibility to be forgiving. And then at Matthew 18, 21 to 35, the story of the servant that owed 10,000 talents, who was forgiven and then went out and jailed his fellow servant who owed 100 denarii, which is a good sum of money, but nowhere near the immense sum that he was forgiven. And so God is very clear that we are to forgive others. However, forgiveness, at least in this life, does not require complete restoration of a broken relationship. I'm thinking of a situation, say, where one spouse is unfaithful. The betrayed spouse would be responsible to forgive their spouse and even the affair partner. But most counselors and therapists would strongly advise that the couple establish a no-contact relationship with the affair partner so they can rebuild their marriage. Likewise, if someone murdered a family member, we should forgive, but that does not mean we're required to have a close relationship with a murderer. Now, in thinking of these examples, these instances, and assuming that everyone involved, the spouses, the affair partner, the murder victim, and the murderer, are all Christian—perhaps even the murderer becomes a Christian because of the forgiveness of the surviving family members—in both the intermediate state where we die and go to be with Christ, and in the final resurrection when God creates a new heaven and a new earth and we dwell with God for eternity, what would be the relationship of these individuals? Perhaps a simple biblical way to put it is, how is Uriah the Hittite going to relate to both King David and Bathsheba? Well, this is a question I've never heard before, and it's somewhat interesting. I think it's not difficult to answer because the problem now for us as Christians is the flesh. And though we are fleshly in this life, even though regenerate, we are not in the flesh in the sense that Paul is referring to in Romans 8 because we are following in the Spirit because we're regenerate, but the flesh is still hanging on, causing us trouble. But that will not be the case when we die. The flesh—actually, the way Paul describes it in some places—is the body of this flesh. I think—did I just read that in Romans 7? The body of this flesh, or maybe Romans 8. But the fleshly tendencies are somehow tied to our body, so that when we die, even in the so-called intermediate state, like where we're disembodied with the Lord until the resurrection when we get our body, the flesh does not come along. When we die, our bodies, physical bodies, our unregenerate bodies, stay behind, lay behind, just disperse. It doesn't trouble us anymore. And so when we are in that state without our bodies, in the Lord, and eventually, of course, with our resurrection bodies, I think all of our relationships are going to be characterized by moral purity. And they will not be affected by whatever the circumstances were beforehand. So if Uriah, the Hittite, who David had murdered because of his adulterous relationship with Uriah's wife, Bathsheba, I don't think that Uriah is going to be holding it against David because everything in our perspective is going to be different. If David is justified in God's eyes, then that's going to be enough for Uriah. And for any of us, let me just back up and put it this way. The fact is, everybody that we know that we will be united with in the afterlife, a uniting that Paul says we should encourage one another with these words, 1 Thess 4, that we're going to be together again. And every one of those relationships, there's compromise. There are hurts, there are hardships, difficulties, and you're right, Ed, forgiveness is really critically important. Got it. And that means we should be forgiving others just as Christ has forgiven us. That's part of the dynamic of a healthy relationship. But guess what? It doesn't happen like that all the time. Some things are never, forgiveness is never requested. Sometimes it's not offered or given because of hurt or pain or it's complicated. But it will not be that way after we die because all of that ugly stuff that corrupts everything in our relationships, that is the flesh, will be gone. And I guess in one sense, if forgiveness is required to resolve the relationship even in the afterlife, that forgiveness will be requested by the person who's the guilty party and needs forgiveness in a relational sense, and it will be given by the person who's been offended because there will be no fleshly inhibitions to interfere with the process of reconciliation in that circumstance. So I don't think it's a big problem, fair question, but all of those kinds of complications in relationships due to our sinful selves and our sinful behaviors, those are all going to be a thing of the fallen past. And thankfully, right? I mean, I don't want to be thinking, gee, how am I going to now when I get to heaven, and we're all going to be there, we've got to fix things because we're not going to be able to escape each other forever. We'll always be together. Let's fix this now. I don't think...

I don't want to worry about that. It is not just that I don't want to worry about that because some things I don't want, I still have to deal with. It's, I don't think we're going to have to deal with it because that's part of the veil of tears, the difficult life that we have to live out in a fallen world, the best we can, even though regenerate. We are not living according to the flesh. We are living according to the Spirit because the Spirit of God dwells within us, Paul's language in Romans 8. But nevertheless, we struggle with the flesh, Paul's acknowledgement in Galatians 5. There we are. The flesh battles against the Spirit and the Spirit against the flesh, so we can't do what we want. One or the other is going to get his way, at least temporarily, and if you're a Christian, you want the spiritual side to have the last say. And, of course, in the resurrection, that battle will be done. So I don't think there's anything to worry about there, Ed, but I appreciate the question. All right? And I was just wondering if Ezekiel's role can be applied to believers today, where if we don't warn non-believers of the judgment that's coming, that we are held responsible for not telling them, even though it's their salvation isn't up to us. So I just appreciate your thoughts on this. Thanks. Well, thanks, Micah. And I've heard variations of this before, and I think we have a sense now what is in the longer passage there, Ezekiel 33, 1 through 9, that you're concerned about. I noticed the language you use. It said Ezekiel is appointed a watchman. I don't have any sense that Christians are appointed as watchmen, as watchmen. Did I just say watchmens? And this is unique to Ezekiel. Now, keep in mind that prophets, Old Testament prophets, were covenant enforcers. Their job was to deal... The nature of their job, the whole point of being a prophet selected by God, appointed by God, was to go to the people and clarify what the people needed to do to be right with God and to exhort and chastise for the behaviors that were not right. And there's a huge record of that. And sometimes the prophets were given the job of encouraging the nation of Israel when the nation was under the kind of judgment that God promised would come to them for not being faithful. In other words, they had a very unique job. And I have no sense that virtually anything that is... I'm going to choose my word carefully here. Endemic or... Maybe that's not the right word. Appropriate or obligatory. Maybe that's the best word. For prophets in their prophetic office regarding Israel, I don't know that anything in that category applies to a Christian. Now, it doesn't mean that we have no... There's no place for us to warn non-Christians of the danger to come. That doesn't mean that we aren't to communicate the opportunities for repentance and forgiveness. It doesn't mean we have no role to play. We do have a role, but the role is defined by the New Testament. And our role isn't the role of a prophet to the world. That was the role of Ezekiel at all to Israel and Judah after the split. But that's because that was their job. Paul was called as an apostle to speak the gospel to the Gentiles. In other words, he was given a very precise, specific kind of responsibility. And that's why Paul says, woe to me if I don't do that. I think that's in Romans 1. Yeah, and woe to anyone, I think, that's a follower of Jesus who has a commission, a unique personal commission by God that doesn't fulfill that. We are obliged to use our gifts, for example. So I'm speaking in the most general sense here. We are obliged to use our gifts. And we need to do that. That's part of our responsibility in the body of Christ. Now, some will have the gift of exhortation, which is kind of an aggressive encouragement. Let's put it that way. And I actually think that's part of my gift mix. I think I have the gift of teaching as well and maybe some other things. I'm not convinced at all that all the gifts are there in 1 Corinthians 12 and Romans 12 and Ephesians 4, et cetera. But I think that they reflect capabilities that we have that God intends for us to use in the life of the church and the spiritual community. And so we're obliged to do that. Peter says in what, 1 Peter, what, 3 or 4? He says, if you've got a spiritual gift, which you do, use it as a good steward of the manifold grace of God. He gives a couple of examples of that. But the point is, use whatever you've got. Get to work. Now, we have an obligation to do that. But the job of a watchman and someone who gives a warning and chastisement to God's people, well, I think that can still be in place somewhat today with the church, although it's the apostles that laid the foundation and the prophets for the church. And now I don't think we have prophets in that sense now, but we certainly do have people with, shall we say, prophetic voices that are there to call Christians back to what they ought to be doing. But I don't think it's the same kind of thing. It's the way they function in the body of Christ. It's not the same as the office of prophet, which was an authoritative office where a person was speaking with authority for God, accomplishing a very particular task with the nation of Israel. In this case, Ezekiel being a watchman. He was actually more than that, as it turns out when you read Ezekiel. But there was this role. And God said, okay, Ezekiel, you'd better do this because if you don't, I'm going to hold you responsible in some sense. I'm not even sure what that would really look like now in a New Testament era where sins are cleansed and forgiven, and that's a given, eternal life for the believer. When there is failure, those failures are still covered by the blood of Christ. It's not an excuse to fail, but they're still covered. So I'm not sure what it would even look like for us now to be held responsible in the same way that Ezekiel might have been. So, simply put, in answer to your question, I don't think that we are in the same circumstance and that God is going to hold us responsible for the people we don't talk to about Christ. God's going to hold them responsible. And they are the ones who are going to have to give an accounting for their life at the great white throne judgment, Revelation 20, and they will be judged according to their deeds that they didn't repent.

or even get the message. I don't think that's going to be on anyone else but them, actually, that they didn't turn to God, they suppressed the truth in unrighteousness. That's what they'll be held responsible for, and worshiped the creature rather than the creator, Romans 1. But they won't be held responsible, we won't be held responsible for their sin. They're judged by their own sin. Any sin that we're guilty of, and maybe we should be speaking up more often than we do. God would want that for us to communicate, and we shrink back when we ought not. Is that a sin? Sure. Validate, comfort, and redirect. So I got this VCR kind of motif that from somewhere about dealing with problems in general. Somebody comes to you with a complaint, validate, comfort, and redirect. And sometimes it's really, really a good model to follow. And it might work in a circumstance like this. And that is to kind of acknowledge the emotions the child is having and validate them. It must be really hard. I can imagine it's difficult with a family where your dad and your mom don't get along well. And then comfort. And then maybe to redirect. And this becomes the harder part. Okay, now what do we do? What can we say? We can do the validate and comfort. Now maybe a redirection is to say, you know, I just want you to know I know you're really troubled by this. So this doesn't mean that your mom and dad don't love each other. All parents squabble sooner or later. So something like that might be offered to redirect. Now notice you're not getting involved or your wife would not be getting involved with the challenge proper, the discussion or the arguments proper, or even the problem proper. At this stage, it's just a matter of managing the youngster who's feeling very strong about this. And it really hurts him to see his parents fighting like this. So affirming that, validating, comforting, then redirecting in the sense that you're saying, this doesn't mean they don't love each other. This happens in relationships. out of the open, this is just my own view on this, so that at least, not in an abusive way, I mean, you want to avoid that regardless, but deal with it somewhat out in the open so that kids could see this is the way families work. That is a question of how do we properly read the text to be able to understand what the author is intending on communicating? And that's the principle key question. What did the author mean? If we're looking at the Bible as some kind of authoritative source of guidance for us in our lives or information about God that we need to be aware of for our relationship with God, et cetera, et cetera, then we need to be clear on what the author is trying to communicate. That's authorial intent. Now, that notion is under attack now. I mean, especially since the postmodern era, it doesn't matter what the text meant to the author. It only matters what it means to you. So you can make up your own meaning and apply it to your own life the way you think you should apply it. This is called deconstruction. At least that's the phrase that is used in the literary sense, deconstructing religion is using the word in a different fashion. And if we think it's somewhat from God, then why don't we care about what God is trying to say, what he's seeking to communicate through the words? That seems to be pretty basic. And that's why authorial intent is so critical when we come to any piece of writing, if we want to be educated about that thing. Now, if we just want to read our own meaning into whatever it is we read, well, then we're not being educated. And you have to know that too, to put the whole package together, because it's harder to really zoom in and tighten down on the authorial intent unless we know to whom that individual is speaking, like a scenario I gave you, the wife of the soldier writing to the soldier that has been away in a dangerous circumstance for, say, a year. Okay, well, now that really helps us understand the rest of what's going on. Genesis 1 is a challenge, and it's not just Genesis 1, but 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, and up until maybe Genesis 12, there's a lot of question about what the heck is going on, especially Genesis 1 through Genesis 2, verse 4, because at verse 4 it seems that there is a shift in the style or approach or something like that. So you're going to have to read, in that case, the whole thing and then start asking a lot of questions. It does seem like something like an historical account is in view here. That seems pretty obvious, but is it the kind of view that is meaning to give a step-by-step process of what took place, or maybe something different? All right. And there are different people who have a different understanding of that. All right. If you think that there is a step-by-step process that is being described, those are called concordists, those who think that there is a concordance between the particular details and the order that they are listed in Genesis 1 and actual history. Well, that's one way of understanding it, but that's not the only way to understand it. By the way, you could be a young earth concordist like Ken Ham, or you could be an old earth concordist like Hugh Ross. They're both concordists. They both are trying to match the details of the text to history. That's certainly a legitimate possibility on either side of that particular fence. But it may be that something else is going on in terms of how it's described. So, to answer your question on how long you have to read, it just depends. And even after you read the whole thing, sometimes it's a little bit more difficult to figure out the intention of the author and what he's trying to do. And you have to look at the genre, certainly, and the method by which he's communicating. There does seem to be a poetic structure of sorts in here, so we want to be careful maybe not to be too literalistic about it. However, it may be that a concordist view, either young earth or old earth, is the right way to approach it. Keep in mind the audience. This is Moses writing to the Jews who have now been brought out of Egypt, where, as a nation, they've been for 400 years. So they have virtually no understanding of their history, the covenant that defines them as a people called the Abrahamic covenant, nor cosmology because the entire cosmology they've been exposed to, as far as we can tell—we don't have a lot of detail about that time, but we do know some things about Egyptian cosmology. And it's clear that in Genesis 1, there are radical contrasts with Egyptian cosmology. For example, and obviously, the sun and the moon do not have names. They have functions. They are not gods. They are things. Now, that's a very important distinction with Egyptian cosmology. And so it's almost as if—this isn't too far of a stretch—Moses is reminding the people of their heritage. He goes all the way back to the beginning and how this is all set up by God. And then he goes into the calling of Abraham and how did these people come to be in the first place and why did they come to be? What is the purpose? That's the Abrahamic covenant. So the audience there is going to be the Jews and the purpose of that Genesis 1 is to tell them about information they don't know and correct things they thought they did know, and the rest of Genesis as well. So how did they get there in Egypt? Well, that's all part of the account. So sometimes, I guess the answer to your question, how long do you have to read to get the author's intent, it just depends. Sometimes you get a general intent very quickly, and then other times you're still trying to figure it out. We know what Paul intends to accomplish in a general sense in writing his epistles, but then when it comes to sections of those letters, we have to see, okay, now what is he getting at here in light of the audience to whom he is writing what is his intention with this paragraph, often called a pericope. Pericope. Pericope. A segment of teaching, kind of like a paragraph, but that you just have to figure out as you move along and kind of keep your options open because whatever is not impossible, given the text, is at least possible. Sometimes it's not plausible, but something seemed to be very straightforward. Other things seem to be odd, a lot of odd things in Scripture, especially the New Testament. And I would say the Gospels. Jesus is odd, and there's a lot of things that Jesus says and I'm not sure what's going on with it. Sometimes the writer, the author of the Gospel, will tell you in the context, here's what Jesus was trying to do, what John chapter 12. And Jesus said, if I be lifted up from the earth, I will draw all men to myself. What the heck is he talking about? Next sentence, this Jesus said, referring to the manner of his death. So when he's lifted up, he means he's going to be lifted up on the cross

and crucified, so there we have the author telling us the intent right away. Now, other times we have, it's a bit more of a mystery. We have to work at figuring this out. Let's hear, we have maybe a short one here. I got about six minutes to go from Johnny. It's the next on my list. It's about communicating with the dead. What do we have there, Johnny? Hello, Greg. This is Johnny from just outside of Richmond, Virginia. I'm a strategic partner and appreciate everything you guys are doing there at STR. Thank you, Johnny. My question for you is, do deceased loved ones communicate to us from the dead? My mom and dad seem to believe that they have seen signs of my sister in strange happenings around the house, and my sister went to be with the Lord four years ago now. And supposedly my parents have even asked questions when these weird happenings were seen, and they saw some kind of response. I'm not aware of any biblical examples that support the idea of deceased loved ones hanging around to communicate with us. I know Jesus told the thief on the cross that he would be with him in heaven that very day, and Jesus didn't say that the thief would hang around earth for a while after he died. Right. And the idea of communicating with the dead seems to be, well, strongly discouraged in multiple places in the Bible. That's an understatement, right. Like in Deuteronomy 18, 10-12, or Saul's experience in 1 Samuel 28. This is, of course, a very delicate topic to bring up with my parents, and I'd like to handle it with gentleness and respect. And to complicate things, while my mom is a Christian and constantly trying to grow deeper in her faith, I'm not sure where my dad stands with his beliefs. I would say my dad is likely not a Christian yet. However, this interaction with what they believe to be my sister's spirit seems to be opening my dad up to the idea of a spiritual realm, and now my dad gives God a foot in the door more and more often in his life. So how does someone trying to be an ambassador for Christ and a loving son confront his parents about this? Whoa, okay. Thank you for your time and advice. Johnny, thank you. And I can see how this is a challenging issue for you. And on a biblical basis, you've got all the goods. You have chapter and verse on everything. There is no biblical evidence that it's anything normal to have communication with the dead. We have one example where that took place. Actually, I guess you might say two examples if you count the transfiguration, but that's a very unique circumstance with Moses and Elijah, you know, they're meeting with Jesus. But Saul's experience in 1 Samuel, I mean, this did not go well for Saul when he tried to conjure up Samuel, okay? Maybe that's 2 Samuel, I'm not sure, but it's not good. He's not supposed to do it, and it was prohibited. The tough job that you have, Johnny, is communicating to your parents about this. Now, I think you could start out on a positive note, and you have, obviously, a mixed crowd. Your mom is a Christian trying to grow spiritually. Your dad, maybe not, maybe at least warming up to spiritual things because he thinks your sister is somehow trying to communicate through paranormal experiences in the home. But I think what I'd want to speak to them both together, if it were up to me, and maybe this isn't the best thing, but there is a spiritual realm, and this is why contact with the dead, at least in principle, is possible. The question is, does God want us to engage in that? And the answer to that is no, and we know this because of prohibitions that he gave the Jews in the law, and you mentioned the passage, Johnny, and also what happened to Saul. Saul broke God's law, and he paid for that with his life, and not just his own life, but the life of his two sons, his sons, maybe more than two, but that included Jonathan, David's close friend. They all died as a result of the father's sin. So I guess I'd want to communicate in a gentle way that, yes, there is a spiritual realm, Dad. You're right about that. It's real, but it isn't all good. And this is why God warns us not to tamper in certain things, and one of them is communicating with the dead. And the only person who did that in the Bible, Samuel, or I should say Saul regarding Samuel, got in a lot of trouble. And we have no confidence, you could say, we have no way to have any confidence that whatever apparition or paranormal activity is going on is actually your sister, Johnny. There is a dark world out there. There's a real spiritual realm, and it's not all good. And I think the reason that God tells us do not mess around with this is because this is an easy avenue for deception. And because oftentimes these circumstances are tied to people who have very, very strong emotions regarding the communication with this other person who has died, they're very, very vulnerable. And you could say, Dad, I'm concerned. Mom, I'm concerned about you. God has told us clearly this is not a good situation. I'm not saying it's not real. I think it could be real. In other words, a real manifestation of something, but I have no reason to believe that it's your sister, even if there's all kinds of stuff given. And the reason is because of what God says. God says no. So that's the way I would approach it, and I think you're in a tough spot, Johnny. You want to be a good son, obviously. Being a good son sometimes is the tough love son. You could do it in a kind and a gracious fashion, but still the issues, it is what it is. It's dangerous. And it's entirely appropriate in the kind of gentle way that I hope I role-played it for you. You can communicate with your parents. Your mom's going to be more responsive probably to the rationale because she's a Christian. Your dad, I don't know. We'll see. But try to pray this one through, and I'm sure you're already doing that. That's the advice I have for you. Thank you, Johnny, and thank you for the rest of you for listening, and I appreciate that. Greg Kokel here for Stand to Reason. Give them heaven. Bye-bye now.

title: Will We Be Held Responsible for Not Warning Non-Believers of the Coming Judgment?
